dataset: Pediatric Rare Disease Structural Mapping v1.1
description: Curated structural sequences, disorder annotations, and disease associations for proteins implicated in rare pediatric genetic disorders including Rett Syndrome, Ewing Sarcoma, and Spinocerebellar Ataxias.
author: James Paul Trageser

targets.name: EWSR1 (EWS RNA-binding protein 1)
targets.disease: Ewing sarcoma (via EWS-FLI1 fusion), desmoplastic small round cell tumor, myxoid liposarcoma
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: EWSR1 is an RNA-binding protein whose N-terminal low-complexity domain (LCD, residues 1-280) undergoes liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS). In Ewing sarcoma — the second most common bone cancer in children and adolescents — the t(11;22)(q24;q12) chromosomal translocation fuses the EWSR1 LCD to the FLI1 ETS transcription factor DNA-binding domain. The resulting EWS-FLI1 fusion oncoprotein acts as an aberrant transcription factor that drives oncogenic gene expression programs from GGAA microsatellite repeats. EWS-FLI1 is intrinsically disordered and lacks a stable 3D structure, making it an exceptionally challenging drug target.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: Direct targeting of EWS-FLI1 remains difficult due to its intrinsic disorder. Current strategies include: (1) TK216, a first-in-class small molecule disrupting EWS-FLI1 interaction with RNA helicase A; (2) targeting downstream effectors like DHX9 with topoisomerase inhibitors; (3) CRISPR-based approaches using GGAA-repeat-driven Cas9 for tumor-specific gene disruption; (4) PROTAC-mediated degradation of EWS-FLI1. 5-year survival for metastatic Ewing sarcoma remains below 30%.
targets.key_residues: LCD/SYGQ-rich domain (1-280): phase separation, transcriptional activation when fused to FLI1; RGG boxes (300-400, 450-530): RNA binding; RRM (360-440): RNA recognition motif; ZnF (518-548): zinc finger
targets.epidemiology: Incidence: ~1 per million children/adolescents per year; peak age 10-15 years; slight male predominance

targets.name: SOX2 (SRY-Box Transcription Factor 2)
targets.disease: SOX2 anophthalmia syndrome (bilateral anophthalmia/microphthalmia), pediatric glioblastoma, medulloblastoma
targets.disorder_level: High (>50% predicted disordered outside HMG box)
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: SOX2 is a master transcription factor essential for maintaining pluripotency in embryonic stem cells and for eye, brain, and nervous system development. Heterozygous loss-of-function mutations cause SOX2 anophthalmia syndrome — children born without eyes or with severely underdeveloped eyes. In pediatric brain tumors, SOX2 is overexpressed and drives cancer stem cell maintenance, therapy resistance, and tumor recurrence. The HMG box domain (40-117) binds the minor groove of DNA and introduces a sharp bend in the double helix.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: For SOX2 anophthalmia: gene therapy approaches to restore SOX2 expression during critical developmental windows. For pediatric CNS tumors: SOX2-targeted immunotherapy, epigenetic silencing strategies, and disruption of SOX2-OCT4 protein-protein interactions. SOX2 is a prognostic biomarker in pediatric glioma.
targets.key_residues: HMG box domain (40-117): DNA binding and bending; Transactivation domain (200-317): transcriptional activation; Nuclear localization signals within HMG box
targets.epidemiology: SOX2 anophthalmia: ~1 in 250,000 births; pediatric glioblastoma expressing SOX2: incidence ~0.85 per 100,000 children

targets.name: MECP2 (Methyl-CpG Binding Protein 2)
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: MECP2 is a chromatin architectural protein that binds methylated CpG dinucleotides and recruits transcriptional co-repressor complexes (NCoR/SMRT). It functions as a master regulator of neuronal gene expression rather than a simple transcriptional silencer. Rett syndrome — affecting approximately 1 in 10,000 female births — is caused by loss-of-function mutations in MECP2 on the X chromosome. Affected girls develop normally for 6-18 months before experiencing regression of speech, hand skills, and social engagement, along with seizures, breathing irregularities, and stereotypic hand movements. The protein requires precise dosage: both deficiency (Rett) and duplication cause severe neurological disease. All functional domains (MBD, TRD, NID) are required for normal neuronal function.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: AAV-based gene therapy (clinical trials by Taysha Gene Therapies, Neurogene) with engineered safety switches to prevent overexpression; exon-skipping strategies boosting MeCP2-E1 isoform production; programmable RNA editing (HG204 for MECP2 duplication syndrome); trofinetide (FDA-approved 2023, first approved treatment for Rett syndrome, targets downstream IGF-1 pathway).
targets.key_residues: MBD (78-162): methyl-CpG binding domain, R106W and R133C are common Rett mutations; TRD (207-310): transcriptional repression domain; NID (285-309): NCoR/SMRT interaction domain; AT-hook motifs: AT-rich DNA binding; R168X, R255X, R270X: most frequent nonsense mutations in Rett syndrome
targets.epidemiology: Rett syndrome: ~1 in 10,000 female births worldwide; MECP2 duplication syndrome: rare, predominantly affects males

targets.name: Ataxin-1
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: Ataxin-1 contains a polyglutamine (polyQ) tract that expands beyond 39 repeats in SCA1 (normal: 6-44 with CAT interruptions; pathogenic: 39-83 pure CAG). Expanded Ataxin-1 misfolds, forms nuclear inclusions in cerebellar Purkinje neurons, and disrupts transcriptional regulation through aberrant interactions with the transcriptional repressor Capicua (CIC) and the RBM17 splicing factor. SCA1 causes progressive cerebellar ataxia, dysarthria, and bulbar dysfunction, typically manifesting in the third or fourth decade. Crucially, nuclear inclusions may be protective rather than toxic — soluble mutant Ataxin-1 in complex with CIC appears to drive neurodegeneration.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: ASO VO659 (Phase 1/2a clinical trial targeting CAG repeat expansion, also covering SCA3 and HD); molecular tweezers (CLR01) inhibiting polyQ aggregation; CIC-Ataxin-1 interaction disruptors; READISCA consortium establishing clinical trial readiness infrastructure.
targets.key_residues: PolyQ tract (~196-234 in normal): expansion trigger; AXH domain (570-689): self-association and Capicua binding; NLS (794-816): nuclear localization; S776: phosphorylation site critical for stability and toxicity
targets.epidemiology: SCA1 prevalence: ~1-2 per 100,000; onset typically 30-40 years but juvenile forms exist with longer expansions

targets.name: Ataxin-3 (Josephin)
targets.pathogenic_mechanism: Ataxin-3 is a deubiquitinating enzyme (DUB) with a catalytic Josephin domain that cleaves K48- and K63-linked polyubiquitin chains. The C-terminal polyQ tract expands from the normal range (12-44 repeats) to pathogenic lengths (55-87 repeats) in SCA3/MJD, the most common dominantly inherited ataxia worldwide. Expanded Ataxin-3 forms intranuclear inclusions in pontine and dentate neurons. The Josephin domain itself contains two aggregation-prone regions that contribute to fibril formation. SCA3/MJD manifests as progressive cerebellar ataxia with pyramidal and extrapyramidal signs, peripheral neuropathy, and external ophthalmoplegia.
targets.therapeutic_relevance: ASO CRD-002 received FDA Orphan Drug Designation in 2025 for SCA3; molecular tweezers CLR01 bind specific lysine residues on Ataxin-3 to inhibit self-assembly; troriluzole NDA accepted with Priority Review for SCA treatment (2025); CRISPR approaches targeting mutant allele.
targets.key_residues: Josephin domain (1-182): deubiquitinase catalytic domain, Cys14 active site; UIM1 (224-243) and UIM2 (244-263): ubiquitin-interacting motifs; PolyQ tract (~292-306 in normal): expansion causes SCA3; NLS (282-285)
targets.epidemiology: SCA3/MJD is the most common autosomal dominant cerebellar ataxia, accounting for ~25-50% of all SCA cases globally; prevalence ~1-5 per 100,000
